text.title: Eval-Driven Development for AI Core Rules
text.summary: The TDD discipline applied to AI features. Skipping any rule reopens the "shipped a regression, found it six weeks later" failure mode this methodology exists to prevent.

rule.statement: Write the eval before the prompt, model, retrieval, or tool change. An eval consists of inputs, expected outputs (or expected properties), a scoring function (deterministic where possible, LLM-judge with rubric otherwise), and a target score. PRs that change a prompt or model without a corresponding eval addition or update are rejected.
rule.rationale: Eval-after-the-fact bakes in the change author's blind spots: the eval only checks what the author noticed during writing. Eval-first forces the author to state the success criterion in measurable form before motivated reasoning takes over.

rule.statement: The eval suite runs in CI on every PR that touches prompts, prompt-rendering code, model configuration, retrieval, tool schemas, or system messages. CI fails the merge when the regression on any stratum exceeds the configured threshold; "looks fine in dev" does not override the gate.
rule.rationale: Without a CI gate the eval is a dashboard, not a discipline. Local-only evals get skipped under deadline pressure, which is precisely when regressions ship.

rule.statement: The eval suite is treated as production code: versioned in the main repo, reviewed in PR, refactored when redundant, owned by a named team. Eval-only PRs are normal; "I just tweaked the eval to pass" without a justification linked to a real product change is rejected at review.
rule.rationale: Eval-as-secondary-artefact decays: nobody cleans up duplicate cases, the rubric drifts, the suite slows down, the team stops trusting it. Treating evals as production code keeps signal value alive across team turnover.

rule.statement: Every prompt or model change is reported with an eval delta line in the PR description: stratum-level before/after on the relevant suite, plus a one-sentence interpretation. Merges without an eval delta line are reopened, not silently approved.
rule.rationale: Without a forced delta line in the PR, regressions on minority strata (the persona that breaks while the average improves) are invisible. The line turns the suite output into a review-time artefact reviewers actually read.

rule.statement: The eval suite has explicit drift watch: monthly review checks that scoring functions still discriminate (not saturated at 100%), that strata still cover real production traffic, that judge models have not silently changed. A suite that loses discrimination on >30% of cases is sunset and rebuilt rather than patched indefinitely.
rule.rationale: Evals that everyone passes are no longer evals; they are decoration. Forcing a sunset criterion prevents teams from carrying around a green suite that has stopped meaning anything, which is the worst form of false confidence.

example.code: PR: tighten system prompt for citation discipline Eval delta (suite: rag-eval-v3, run 2026-05-20): - overall: 0.81 → 0.86 (+0.05) - stratum "long-form answer": 0.72 → 0.81 (+0.09) - stratum "code-block answer": 0.91 → 0.86 (-0.05) ← regression, mitigation: added two code-cite cases to suite, scored 0.86 → 0.90 after fix. CI: rag-eval gate passed (threshold ≥0.83 overall, ≥0.85 on code stratum).
PR: improved prompt Tested: looks good in spot-check.
example.note: No eval, no stratum view, no gate — the regression on the code-block stratum ships and the team learns from a customer complaint.
